sections.plain_summary: Functional dyspepsia (FD) is indigestion without an obvious cause like ulcers or reflux disease. It involves recurring discomfort or pain in the upper stomach, often triggered by eating. The exact cause is unclear, but it may involve how the stomach moves food, sensitivity to normal sensations, or gut bacteria.
sections.detailed_summary: Functional dyspepsia is a common disorder characterised by chronic or recurrent symptoms in the upper abdomen—such as early fullness, bloating, belching, nausea, or epigastric pain—that occur in the absence of organic disease (ulcers, inflammation, or malignancy). Symptoms typically begin or worsen after eating. The pathophysiology is heterogeneous and may involve impaired gastric accommodation, delayed gastric emptying, visceral hypersensitivity, low-grade inflammation, alterations in gut microbiota, or psychological factors. It is diagnosed clinically after exclusion of alarm features (e.g. weight loss, vomiting, anaemia) and often after a brief course of acid suppression; endoscopy is not routinely required unless red flags are present.

prevalence.summary: Functional dyspepsia affects roughly 1 in 5 to 1 in 4 people globally, making it one of the most common gastrointestinal complaints.
prevalence.estimate: 15–25% of the population; 5–10% meet diagnostic criteria for FD
prevalence.notes: Prevalence varies by geography and diagnostic criteria applied. Many people with mild symptoms do not seek care. Female predominance is modest (1.1:1 to 1.3:1). Onset is often in young adulthood, but can occur at any age. Rates are higher in healthcare-seeking populations.

subgroups.name: Postprandial distress syndrome (PDS)
subgroups.description: Symptoms predominantly triggered or worsened by eating; characterised by early satiety and postprandial fullness.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms occur after normal-sized or small meals
Often associated with impaired gastric accommodation or delayed emptying

subgroups.name: Epigastric pain syndrome (EPS)
subgroups.description: Symptoms dominated by epigastric pain or burning, which may or may not be meal-related.
distinguishing_features: Less consistent relationship to food intake
May overlap with reflux-like or ulcer-like presentations

subgroups.name: Overlapping/mixed presentations
subgroups.description: Many patients have features of both PDS and EPS simultaneously.
distinguishing_features: Both postprandial fullness and epigastric pain
May respond differently to different treatments

symptoms.symptom: Early satiety (feeling full quickly after starting to eat)
symptoms.notes: Often one of the earliest and most bothersome symptoms; may lead to reduced food intake and weight loss in severe cases
Typically occurs 30 minutes to 2 hours after eating; severity may not correlate with actual meal size
May be dull, sharp, or burning; can be meal-related or occur between meals; often worse in the evening or after large meals
May occur after eating or independently; rarely progresses to vomiting in FD alone
Often reflexive; may be related to air swallowing (aerophagia) or impaired gastric venting

symptoms.symptom: Bloating (with or without visible abdominal distension)
symptoms.notes: Subjective sensation of bloating may be more prominent than objective distension; worsens throughout the day

symptoms.symptom: Loss of appetite or reduced food intake
symptoms.notes: Secondary to early satiety or pain; may lead to nutritional concerns if severe
Usually in the epigastrium or upper abdomen; may be relieved or worsened by eating or bowel movements

symptoms.symptom: Vague upper abdominal heaviness after eating
symptoms.notes: Sensation of 'weight' in the stomach; distinct from pain but equally bothersome

key_patterns: Symptoms triggered or worsened by eating, particularly after moderately large or high-fat meals
Absence of alarm features (weight loss, persistent vomiting, anaemia, dysphagia) that would suggest organic disease
Symptoms lasting at least 3 months with onset at least 6 months before diagnosis
Normal or non-specific findings on endoscopy or imaging (if performed)
Possible association with stress, anxiety, or mood changes

at_home.name: Symptom diary
at_home.description: Record the timing, severity (0–10 scale), type of food eaten, portion size, and any associated stressors for 1–2 weeks. Note which meals trigger symptoms, whether certain foods are worse, and any patterns with stress, sleep, or menstrual cycle. Look for meal-related onset or worsening, even if variability is high.
at_home.safety_notes: This is a low-risk observation tool. Keeping a diary may heighten awareness of symptoms, which can be helpful but may also increase anxiety in some people; use it as a tool, not a source of worry.

at_home.name: Meal size and composition test
at_home.description: For 3–5 days, try eating smaller, more frequent meals with lower fat content. Record onset time of symptoms and severity. FD often improves with smaller portions and lower-fat foods, whereas other conditions (e.g. GORD) may show different patterns.
at_home.safety_notes: This is safe and often therapeutic. If symptoms worsen dramatically or you develop new red-flag symptoms (persistent vomiting, weight loss, blood in stool), seek clinical evaluation.

at_home.name: Stress and symptom correlation
at_home.description: Note whether symptoms cluster around times of high stress, poor sleep, or anxiety. Track mood or stress level alongside GI symptoms for 2 weeks. Psychological factors often exacerbate FD.
at_home.safety_notes: Do not use this to convince yourself that symptoms are 'just in your head'; FD is real and involves measurable physiological changes. The aim is to identify modifiable triggers.

clinical.name: Upper endoscopy (oesophagogastroduodenoscopy, OGD)
clinical.description: Visual inspection of the oesophagus, stomach, and first part of the small intestine via a thin camera. Allows direct visualisation and biopsy if needed. In FD, endoscopy is typically normal unless alarm features are present.
clinical.availability: Ordered by primary care or gastroenterology; performed in endoscopy units (hospital or private clinic). May be done if red flags exist or symptoms are severe.

clinical.name: Gastric emptying study (scintigraphy)
clinical.description: Measures how long food (labelled with a radioactive tracer) takes to leave the stomach. Delayed gastric emptying is present in ~30% of FD patients. Performed by swallowing a labelled meal followed by imaging at timed intervals over 1–4 hours.
clinical.availability: Ordered by gastroenterology; performed in nuclear medicine departments. Less commonly used now unless gastroparesis is suspected or symptoms are severe.

clinical.name: Gastric accommodation test (SPECT or 3D ultrasound)
clinical.description: Measures the stomach's ability to relax and expand after a meal. Impaired accommodation is found in ~40% of FD patients. SPECT uses a radioactive tracer; 3D ultrasound is non-invasive but less standardised.
clinical.availability: Specialised centres only; not widely available. Primarily a research tool.

clinical.name: Upper abdominal ultrasound or CT
clinical.description: Imaging to exclude structural abnormalities (e.g. gallstones, pancreatic disease). Non-invasive and quick, but low sensitivity for functional motility disorders. Useful to rule out other causes.
clinical.availability: Ordered by primary care or gastroenterology; performed in radiology departments.

clinical.name: H. pylori serology or breath/stool testing
clinical.description: Tests for Helicobacter pylori infection, which can cause or mimic FD. A positive result warrants eradication therapy. Serology detects antibodies (past or current infection); breath or stool tests detect active infection.
clinical.availability: Ordered by primary care or gastroenterology; blood test, breath test, or stool test performed in primary care or laboratory.

clinical.name: Coeliac serology (tTG-IgA)
clinical.description: Screens for coeliac disease, which can present with dyspepsia and may co-exist with FD. A positive result requires confirmation with endoscopic biopsy.
clinical.availability: Ordered by primary care or gastroenterology; blood test performed in primary care or laboratory.

clinical.name: Full blood count and basic metabolic panel
clinical.description: Checks for anaemia (suggesting chronic bleeding or malabsorption) and electrolyte abnormalities. Reassuring if normal in the absence of alarming symptoms.
clinical.availability: Ordered by primary care; blood test performed in primary care or laboratory.

related_conditions.name: Gastroparesis
related_conditions.explanation: Delayed gastric emptying occurs in ~30% of FD patients and may be a primary driver of early satiety and postprandial fullness. Gastroparesis (severe delayed emptying) is a distinct but overlapping disorder.

related_conditions.name: H. pylori infection
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori can cause or exacerbate dyspepsia. Testing and eradication (if positive) is a standard first step; some FD may resolve post-eradication, though many persist.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation
related_conditions.explanation: Visceral hypersensitivity—heightened perception of normal stomach sensations—is a core feature in many FD patients, similar to central sensitisation in other chronic pain conditions.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Anxiety and FD frequently co-occur and bidirectionally influence each other. Anxiety increases symptom perception and may impair gastric function via gut–brain axis pathways.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Depression and FD are commonly comorbid. Depressive symptoms are associated with symptom severity, poor outcomes, and reduced treatment response.

related_conditions.name: Trauma / PTSD
related_conditions.explanation: Early-life adversity, abuse, or trauma are associated with increased FD risk and severity via dysregulation of stress response and gut–brain signalling.

related_conditions.name: SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth can mimic FD with bloating, early fullness, and discomfort. Some FD patients may have concurrent SIBO, though causality is debated.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Altered gut microbiota composition and function are implicated in FD pathophysiology. Dysbiosis may contribute to inflammation, fermentation, and altered motility.

related_conditions.name: Hypermobile EDS / HSD
related_conditions.explanation: Connective tissue disorders (particularly hEDS) are associated with gastric dysmotility and increased prevalence of FD-like symptoms.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: Dysautonomic features (autonomic dysfunction) may impair gastric innervation and motility, increasing FD risk in autonomic disorder populations.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Mast cell-mediated inflammation and mediator release may contribute to visceral hypersensitivity and dysmotility in some FD patients.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Impaired histamine degradation may worsen FD symptoms via mast cell activation and increased intestinal permeability in susceptible individuals.

related_conditions.name: IgG food sensitivities
related_conditions.explanation: IgG testing is not validated for FD diagnosis, but some patients attribute symptoms to food sensitivities. True food allergy or intolerance can co-exist but is distinct from FD.

related_conditions.name: Lactase deficiency
related_conditions.explanation: Lactose intolerance can coexist with and mimic FD; testing or trial elimination may help clarify if dairy is a trigger.

related_conditions.name: Fructose malabsorption
related_conditions.explanation: Fructose malabsorption can cause bloating and early fullness similar to FD; may coexist and require separate dietary management.

related_conditions.name: Hormonal / menstrual cycle effects
related_conditions.explanation: FD symptoms often fluctuate with the menstrual cycle in women; oestrogen and progesterone modulate gastric motility and visceral sensitivity.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Hypothyroidism can impair gastric motility and cause dyspepsia-like symptoms; should be screened and ruled out.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease can present with upper GI symptoms similar to FD; serological screening is recommended to exclude this treatable cause.

strategies.name: Dietary modification: smaller, more frequent meals
strategies.description: Eat 5–6 smaller meals per day (200–300 kcal each) rather than 2–3 large ones. Smaller portions reduce the stimulus for early satiety and reduce postprandial discomfort. Distribute eating throughout the day to maintain adequate intake.
strategies.notes: This is often the first-line dietary intervention and is safe. Effectiveness varies; some patients improve significantly, others require additional measures. May need to time meals around work or activity schedules.

strategies.name: Dietary modification: reduce fat intake
strategies.description: Lower fat content, particularly saturated and fried fats, as these slow gastric emptying and trigger symptoms. Aim for <30–40% of calories from fat. Choose lean proteins, low-fat dairy, and healthy oils in moderation.
strategies.notes: High-fat foods are the strongest meal-related trigger. Gradual reduction often works better than abrupt elimination. Monitor overall nutrition.

strategies.name: Dietary modification: avoid triggers
strategies.description: Common triggers include: spicy foods, caffeine, alcohol, carbonated beverages, and chocolate. Keep a diary to identify personal triggers and eliminate or reduce them systematically. Not all trigger foods are universal; individualisation is key.
strategies.notes: Triggers vary widely between individuals. Elimination should be evidence-based (i.e. documented in diary) rather than arbitrary. Reintroduce after 2–4 weeks to confirm causality.

strategies.name: Eat slowly and chew thoroughly
strategies.description: Chew food well (20–30 times per mouthful) and eat slowly (20–30 minutes for a meal). This aids mechanical digestion, reduces air swallowing, and allows for earlier satiety signals to reach the brain.
strategies.notes: This is a simple, cost-free intervention. Effectiveness requires consistent effort and behavioural change. May be harder in busy environments.

strategies.name: Avoid lying down immediately after meals
strategies.description: Remain upright for 2–3 hours after eating. This uses gravity to aid stomach emptying and reduces reflux-like symptoms and bloating.
strategies.notes: Particularly important for evening meals. Even gentle activity (standing, walking) is beneficial.

strategies.name: Proton pump inhibitor (PPI) trial (short-term)
strategies.description: A 4-week course of omeprazole, lansoprazole, or similar PPI to suppress acid production. Used diagnostically: if symptoms resolve, acid-related disease may be present; if not, FD is more likely. Also reduces epigastric pain in EPS subtype.
strategies.notes: First-line pharmacological approach. Long-term PPI use is associated with risks (B12 deficiency, bone loss, C. difficile). Discontinue after 4 weeks if not clearly beneficial and reassess diagnosis.

strategies.name: H2-receptor antagonist (ranitidine, famotidine)
strategies.description: Reduces gastric acid. Less potent than PPIs but often well-tolerated. May help with epigastric pain or heartburn-like symptoms.
strategies.notes: Ranitidine was withdrawn in 2020 due to contamination concerns; famotidine and cimetidine remain available. Not as effective as PPIs for FD alone.

strategies.name: Prokinetic agents (domperidone, metoclopramide)
strategies.description: Enhance gastric muscle contractions to speed emptying. Domperidone is preferred (fewer CNS side effects); metoclopramide is a stronger dopamine antagonist but carries risk of tardive dyskinesia with long-term use. Typically used for 4–12 weeks.
strategies.notes: Modest efficacy in FD; more beneficial if gastric emptying is documented as slow. Metoclopramide is reserved for short-term use due to neurological risks. May lose efficacy over time (tachyphylaxis).

strategies.name: Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline, nortriptyline)
strategies.description: Low-dose tricyclics (10–50 mg daily at bedtime) reduce visceral hypersensitivity and improve global FD symptoms, independent of mood. Mechanism includes anticholinergic effects and analgesic properties.
strategies.notes: Often effective for pain-predominant FD (EPS). Side effects include dry mouth, sedation, weight gain, and constipation. Start low and titrate slowly. Takes 2–4 weeks to show benefit.

strategies.name: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI)
strategies.description: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) may help if anxiety or depression is prominent. Improvement often reflects treatment of comorbid mood disorder rather than direct FD effect.
strategies.notes: Less evidence than tricyclics for FD alone. Consider if significant anxiety or depression is present. Takes 4–6 weeks to show effect.

strategies.name: Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT)
strategies.description: Structured psychological intervention addressing thoughts, beliefs, and behaviours related to GI symptoms. Reduces symptom amplification, improves coping, and may improve symptom perception and distress.
strategies.notes: Most effective when anxiety, catastrophising, or health anxiety is prominent. Benefits persist after therapy ends. Requires motivated engagement and trained therapist availability.

strategies.name: Gut-directed hypnotherapy
strategies.description: Guided relaxation and hypnotic suggestion focussed on the GI tract. May reduce visceral hypersensitivity and improve global symptoms. Sessions typically occur weekly for 8–12 weeks.
strategies.notes: Strong evidence in IBS; emerging evidence in FD. Requires a trained practitioner. Effective for some but not all patients; best for those open to the approach.

strategies.name: Stress reduction and relaxation techniques
strategies.description: Mindfulness, progressive muscle relaxation, or other stress-reduction practices performed daily. Reduces physiological stress response and gut–brain signalling dysregulation. Even 10–15 minutes daily is beneficial.
strategies.notes: Low-risk, inexpensive, and widely accessible (apps, online resources). Effectiveness varies; some people find it very helpful, others require structured therapy.

strategies.name: Regular physical activity
strategies.description: Moderate aerobic exercise (30 minutes, 3–5 times per week) or gentle movement (walking, yoga). Improves gastric motility, reduces stress, and enhances overall GI function.
strategies.notes: Should be mild to moderate; intense or high-impact exercise may worsen symptoms in some. Walking after meals is particularly beneficial.

strategies.name: Improve sleep quality and duration
strategies.description: Aim for 7–9 hours, maintain a regular sleep schedule, and optimise sleep environment (dark, cool, quiet). Poor sleep exacerbates FD symptoms and impairs stress regulation.
strategies.notes: Sleep and GI symptoms are bidirectionally linked. Often requires behavioural change (sleep hygiene) or medical management of sleep disorders.

strategies.name: Peppermint oil
strategies.description: Enteric-coated peppermint oil (0.2–0.4 mL, 3 times daily) may reduce bloating, discomfort, and motility dysfunction. Works via smooth muscle relaxation. Typical trial is 4–8 weeks.
strategies.notes: Some evidence in IBS and FD. Generally well-tolerated; may cause heartburn if not enteric-coated. Not first-line but reasonable to try if dietary measures are insufficient.

strategies.name: Ginger
strategies.description: Fresh or powdered ginger (1–2 g daily, divided) may improve gastric motility and reduce nausea and bloating. Often used as a tea or in food.
strategies.notes: Limited robust evidence in FD specifically, but long traditional use and general safety. May interact with medications; use with caution if on blood thinners.

strategies.name: Probiotics
strategies.description: Specific probiotic strains (e.g. Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium) may reduce bloating and improve symptoms by altering gut microbiota composition. Typical trial is 4–12 weeks.
strategies.notes: Evidence is mixed and strain-specific. Not all probiotics are equal. Efficacy varies widely between individuals. Consider if dysbiosis is suspected or after antibiotics.

strategies.name: Fermented foods
strategies.description: Incorporate fermented foods (sauerkraut, kefir, tempeh, miso) gradually to promote beneficial bacteria. May improve digestive tolerance over time.
strategies.notes: Generally safe and may support microbiota diversity. Introduce slowly; fermented foods can initially increase bloating if dysbiosis is severe.

things_to_avoid: Large meals: they trigger early satiety, pain, and bloating more than small, frequent eating does
High-fat foods: they are the strongest meal-related trigger and slow gastric emptying significantly
Caffeine and alcohol: both stimulate acid production and impair stomach function in susceptible people
Carbonated and fizzy drinks: they increase bloating and abdominal distension
Spicy foods: they irritate the gastric lining and trigger pain in many FD patients
Lying down or bending over immediately after eating: this impairs stomach emptying and worsens symptoms
Eating too quickly or without chewing: it increases air swallowing and overwhelms the stomach, worsening early satiety
Chronic long-term PPI or NSAID use without monitoring: both carry risks (nutrient malabsorption, side effects) and may not address underlying FD mechanisms
Rumination about symptoms or health anxiety: this amplifies symptom perception and perpetuates the cycle of distress
Rigid or overly restrictive diets: they often exacerbate anxiety and may worsen nutritional status without clear symptom benefit

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Functional dyspepsia is not curable by medication alone. Medications manage symptoms temporarily, but once you stop them, symptoms typically return unless underlying triggers are addressed.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients hope for a 'pill' to fix the problem and want quick relief without lifestyle change. The term 'functional' is often misheard as meaning 'not real,' leading patients to seek a magic cure rather than accept ongoing self-management.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Engaged patients learn to identify and modify their specific triggers (meal size, fat content, stress, sleep) and build sustainable habits. They move from symptom suppression to symptom prevention, which requires effort but gives them control and reduces medication dependence over time.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Your gut and brain are strongly connected. FD is not 'all in your head,' but psychological factors—stress, anxiety, catastrophising—genuinely worsen stomach function. You cannot will your stomach to work better, but you can reduce the psychological load that makes it worse.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients fear being told their symptoms are psychosomatic or 'not real.' Acknowledging anxiety's role feels like admitting defeat or accepting blame. Many resist psychological treatment because they see it as invalidating their physical symptoms.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who accept the gut–brain connection often find that managing stress, sleep, and mood—via therapy, meditation, or lifestyle change—reduces symptoms more reliably than medication alone. They stop waiting for a diagnosis to change and start actively managing the interaction between their thoughts, stress, and stomach.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: FD is variability. Symptoms will fluctuate. Some days are worse than others, and this is normal. Seeking a cause for every fluctuation or expecting a straight line of improvement will drive you mad.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients want predictability and a clear cause-and-effect link. They search for the 'one thing' that triggered today's symptoms and become frustrated when the same meal is fine one day and terrible the next. This searching amplifies symptom focus.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who accept variability shift their attention from 'Why today?' to 'How can I cope today?' They recognise that multiple factors (stress, sleep, gut inflammation, hormones, meal composition) interact unpredictably. This acceptance reduces anxiety and allows them to focus on modifiable factors rather than chasing phantom triggers.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: If you have been diagnosed with FD after normal endoscopy and basic tests, further expensive testing (advanced motility studies, imaging, specialised blood work) is unlikely to change your management. The diagnosis is clinical, and your improvement depends on trying the interventions that work for most people, not on identifying a rare cause.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients feel there 'must be something,' so they pursue more tests hoping for an explanation. They delay starting treatment because they fear treating symptoms without knowing the 'real' cause. Fear of missed diagnosis drives testing cycles.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who engage with evidence-based FD management (dietary change, stress management, targeted medication if needed) often see meaningful improvement within 4–12 weeks. They stop waiting for the perfect diagnosis and start treating the problem that exists now.

key_levers.lever: Establish consistent meal size and composition (small, low-fat, frequent meals)
key_levers.why_it_matters: This is the single most modifiable trigger for FD. Large, fatty meals directly impair stomach function and trigger early satiety, bloating, and pain. Consistency reduces day-to-day variability and allows other factors (stress, sleep) to stabilise.
key_levers.how_to_start: Choose one meal per day—usually the largest (often dinner)—and reduce portion by 30–40%. Replace some calories with an extra snack 2–3 hours later. Keep a simple log of meal size and symptom timing. After 1 week, adjust the next largest meal. Within 3 weeks, you'll see if this is your main lever.
key_levers.timeline: Symptom improvement often begins within 3–7 days; significant improvement by 2–4 weeks if this is a major trigger.

key_levers.lever: Identify and reduce dietary fat, especially from trigger foods
key_levers.why_it_matters: Fat is the most potent slower of gastric emptying. Reducing fat from ~40% of calories to 25–30% improves stomach emptying and reduces postprandial bloating in most people.
key_levers.how_to_start: For 1 week, note all high-fat foods you eat (butter, oil, fatty meats, cheese, cream, fried foods). Replace three of them with lower-fat alternatives. For example: use low-fat yoghurt instead of full-fat, grilled chicken instead of fried, olive oil spray instead of butter. Start small—don't overhaul diet overnight.
key_levers.timeline: Noticeable improvement often appears within 1–2 weeks. Optimal benefit takes 4 weeks as the stomach adapts.

key_levers.lever: Manage stress and sleep actively (not passively)
key_levers.why_it_matters: Poor sleep and chronic stress directly impair gastric motility and increase visceral hypersensitivity. These factors drive 30–50% of FD symptom variation. No dietary or medication change will succeed if stress and sleep are neglected.
key_levers.how_to_start: Pick one small stress-management tool: 10 minutes of walking daily, 5 minutes of box breathing (4-in, 4-hold, 4-out) twice daily, or one 20-minute yoga session weekly. For sleep, pick one sleep habit: no screens 1 hour before bed, or a consistent bedtime ±30 min, or a cool, dark bedroom. Start with ONE, master it for 2 weeks, then add a second.
key_levers.timeline: Sleep quality improves within 1 week; stress-related GI symptoms often follow within 2–3 weeks. Full benefit takes 4–8 weeks.

key_levers.lever: Address anxiety or depression if present
key_levers.why_it_matters: Comorbid anxiety and depression predict worse FD outcomes and amplify symptom perception. Treating mood often improves GI symptoms, even if medication is weak. Psychological therapy can be as effective as medication.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss mood and anxiety screening with your primary care provider. If present, ask about low-dose tricyclic antidepressant (amitriptyline 10–25 mg at bedtime) or referral to CBT. Even starting therapy (rather than waiting for therapy availability) begins the process.
key_levers.timeline: Medication effect takes 2–4 weeks; therapy effects take 6–8 weeks but are more durable. Combined approach is strongest.

key_levers.lever: Test for and treat H. pylori if positive
key_levers.why_it_matters: H. pylori eradication resolves FD in a minority of people but is essential to rule out. A positive result changes management decisively.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ask your primary care provider for H. pylori serology or breath/stool test if not already done. If positive, pursue a standard eradication course (PPI + two antibiotics for 7–14 days). Retest 4 weeks after completion to confirm eradication.
key_levers.timeline: Test results in 3–7 days; eradication takes 1–2 weeks; symptom response (if any) takes 4–8 weeks post-eradication.

key_levers.lever: Trial of tricyclic antidepressant (amitriptyline) if pain or sensitivity is prominent
key_levers.why_it_matters: Low-dose tricyclics reduce visceral pain and improve global FD symptoms in 40–50% of patients, particularly for pain-predominant (EPS) subtype. Effect is independent of mood benefits.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ask your primary care provider or gastroenterologist about starting amitriptyline 10 mg at bedtime (or nortriptyline if amitriptyline is not tolerated). Increase by 10 mg every 3–7 days to target dose of 25–50 mg. Set a 4-week review to assess benefit.
key_levers.timeline: Mild benefit may appear within 2 weeks; full benefit takes 4–8 weeks. Requires trial period of at least 4–6 weeks to fairly assess.

common_mistakes: Chasing a diagnosis instead of starting treatment: Excessive testing delays evidence-based management and increases healthcare costs and anxiety. FD is a clinical diagnosis; start dietary and lifestyle changes while waiting for results.

Eliminating too many foods at once: Overly restrictive diets worsen nutritional status and anxiety without clearly identifying triggers. Eliminate one trigger at a time, confirm benefit, then move to the next.

Expecting linear improvement: FD is variable. A bad day does not mean treatment failed; focus on the trend over 2–4 weeks, not day-to-day fluctuation. Expecting perfection increases frustration and discontinuation of helpful strategies.

Stopping medication or lifestyle change too early: Many people see initial benefit, then stop the intervention (e.g. small meals) and relapse. True improvement requires sustained behaviour change for at least 4–8 weeks; benefits are often conditional on continuing the strategy.
Neglecting stress and sleep while pursuing medication: No medication will work optimally if sleep is poor and stress is unmanaged. Medication + lifestyle is far more effective than either alone.

Using medication alone without dietary change: PPIs, H2 blockers, and prokinetics provide symptomatic relief but do not address meal-related triggers. Combining medication with smaller, lower-fat meals is more effective than medication alone.

Ruminating on symptoms and becoming hypervigilant: Constant symptom monitoring amplifies perception and anxiety, worsening symptoms. Set specific symptom check-in times (e.g. note bloating once daily at dinner) rather than all-day monitoring.
Blaming yourself or feeling shame: FD is not caused by eating wrong or being 'weak.' It is a real physiological disorder with multiple drivers. Self-blame reduces engagement and perpetuates anxiety.

specialists.type: General practitioner (GP) or primary care physician
specialists.role: Often the first point of contact. Can perform initial assessment, exclude red flags (anaemia, weight loss, vomiting), test for H. pylori and coeliac disease, and prescribe first-line treatments (PPI, prokinetics). Can refer to gastroenterology or mental health services as needed.
specialists.notes: Some GPs are very knowledgeable about FD management; others may under-recognise it or over-investigate. Clear, specific communication about symptoms and impact is helpful.

specialists.role: Specialist in digestive diseases. Performs upper endoscopy if indicated, diagnoses complicated FD (rule out organic disease), and initiates advanced treatments (prokinetics, tricyclic antidepressants). Often the most familiar with FD diagnostic criteria and management.
specialists.notes: Often the first gastroenterology referral; most FD is managed in primary care once diagnosed. A gastroenterologist experienced in functional disorders is ideal; some focus primarily on organic disease and may be less familiar with FD nuances.

specialists.role: Focuses on underlying mechanisms (nutrient absorption, microbiota, food triggers, stress) and individualised treatment plans. May spend more time on dietary assessment and lifestyle factors than conventional gastroenterology. Often more willing to explore complementary approaches.
specialists.notes: Quality and evidence-base vary widely. Some practise evidence-based functional medicine; others promote unvalidated tests or supplements. Verify credentials and avoid practitioners dismissing conventional diagnosis or promoting unnecessary testing.

specialists.role: Provides cognitive-behavioural therapy or other psychological interventions to address anxiety, catastrophising, stress, and gut–brain dysregulation. Evidence-based and often highly effective, particularly for anxiety-driven FD.
specialists.notes: May be under-utilised in FD management. Ideally someone with experience in GI conditions or chronic pain. Ask whether they are trained in gut-directed therapy or have experience with FD.

specialists.role: Provides structured dietary assessment, identifies specific food triggers, and designs a sustainable, nutritionally balanced eating plan tailored to FD (meal size, composition, timing). Helps avoid unnecessary elimination diets.
specialists.notes: Often under-referred for FD despite strong evidence supporting dietary management. A dietitian with experience in GI conditions is ideal. Ask whether they work in collaboration with your GI doctor.

specialists.role: Delivers hypnotherapy specifically designed for GI symptoms, helping reduce visceral hypersensitivity and improve symptom control. Evidence is emerging but shows promise in FD.
specialists.notes: Less readily available than other practitioners. Verify training and certification in gut-directed therapy; general hypnotherapy may be less effective for FD. Often used as a complementary approach alongside medical management.

patient_communities.name: ROME Foundation (International Foundation for Functional Gastrointestinal Disorders)
patient_communities.notes: Official source of ROME diagnostic criteria and evidence-based FD information. Excellent patient education materials, expert blogs, and research summaries. Not a community forum, but authoritative and balanced information.

patient_communities.name: IBS Network / IBS Symptom Tracker
patient_communities.notes: UK-based charity with resources for FD and IBS overlap. Forum and app for symptom tracking. Practical, peer-reviewed content and supportive community.

patient_communities.name: National Functional Neurogastroenterology and Motility Society (NFNMS) / Patient Support Resources
patient_communities.notes: Professional organisation with some patient-facing resources. Useful for finding specialists and accessing research summaries on FD and motility disorders.

patient_communities.name: Reddit: r/FunctionalDyspepsia
patient_communities.notes: Active, supportive community where people share symptoms, dietary findings, medication experiences, and coping strategies. Peer-driven; quality varies but generally evidence-informed. Good for real-world symptom patterns.

patient_communities.name: Facebook groups: Functional Dyspepsia Support
patient_communities.notes: Multiple active groups with thousands of members sharing experiences, recipes, and tips. Often very supportive but variable medical accuracy; approach anecdotes as observations, not proof.

patient_communities.name: Inspire: Digestive Health Community
patient_communities.notes: Moderated patient communities for various GI conditions, including FD. Peer support with professional moderation. Good balance of empathy and accuracy.

patient_communities.name: Functional Gut Network (FODMAP Diet and Beyond)
patient_communities.notes: Focuses on low-FODMAP diet and functional GI disorders. Not FD-specific but valuable for those with overlapping IBS or bloating/fermentation concerns. Dietitian-led content.

learning_resources.title: The Rome Foundation Functional Dyspepsia Patient Guide
learning_resources.description: Official, evidence-based guide on FD, including cause, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment options. Written for patients in accessible language, but comprehensive and medically accurate.

learning_resources.title: Gut: The Inside Story of Our Body's Most Underrated Organ
learning_resources.description: Accessible, illustrated introduction to how the digestive system works, including gut-brain connections, the microbiome, and common GI issues. Entertaining and informative without being overly technical.

learning_resources.title: The Whole Body Approach to Clinical Obesity and Weight Regain: Impact of Gastrointestinal Dysfunction
learning_resources.description: Provides mechanistic insight into how FD and gastric dysfunction impair eating and weight management. For those interested in deeper understanding of pathophysiology.

learning_resources.title: Functional Dyspepsia and the Microbiome: What the Evidence Tells Us
learning_resources.author_or_source: Various, available in Clinical and Translational Gastroenterology
learning_resources.description: Reviews emerging evidence on gut dysbiosis and SIBO in FD, including probiotics and dietary approaches. Technical but important for understanding FD's complex aetiology.

learning_resources.title: Full Catastrophe Living: Using the Wisdom of Your Body and Mind to Face Stress, Pain, and Illness
learning_resources.description: Foundational text on mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) and the mind–body connection. Highly relevant for FD's psychological and stress components. Practical, research-backed, and accessible.

learning_resources.title: The Gut Feeling: Psychosomatic Aspects of Functional Gastrointestinal Disorders
learning_resources.description: Explains the gut–brain axis, how stress exacerbates FD, and why psychological intervention is not 'giving up' but essential management. Helps reframe the mind–body connection.

learning_resources.title: CBT for Irritable Bowel Syndrome and Functional Dyspepsia
learning_resources.author_or_source: Various, searchable via PubMed or Google Scholar
learning_resources.description: Reviews evidence for cognitive-behavioural therapy in FD, including specific techniques and expected outcomes. For those considering or beginning psychological treatment.

learning_resources.title: Low FODMAP Diet for Functional Dyspepsia: What the Evidence Shows
learning_resources.description: Critically examines low-FODMAP diet in FD (evidence is modest); useful for those considering this diet to understand its place in FD management.

learning_resources.title: Functional Dyspepsia: Advances in Diagnosis and Treatment
learning_resources.author_or_source: GI Society, American College of Gastroenterology
learning_resources.description: Expert interviews and talks on FD diagnosis, treatment options, and emerging research. Accessible multimedia format for learning.

learning_resources.title: The Mind–Gut Connection: How the Hidden Conversation Within Our Bodies Impacts Mental Health and Shapesour Futures
learning_resources.description: Deep dive into the neurobiology of the gut–brain axis, written by a leading researcher in the field. Science-based and accessible; important for understanding why FD is more than just 'dysmotility.'

learning_resources.title: ACG Clinical Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of Dyspepsia
learning_resources.description: Official, evidence-based clinical guideline for FD diagnosis and management, targeted at healthcare professionals but readable for informed patients. Summarises current standard of care.

learning_resources.title: ROME IV Criteria for Functional Dyspepsia
learning_resources.description: Diagnostic criteria used worldwide for FD. Understanding these helps clarify whether your symptoms meet the definition and what to expect from medical evaluation.

sections.relevance_signal: **Pain or fullness in the upper stomach** (not lower), early satiety, or burning — without an ulcer or H. pylori.
